name: Trinity
purpose: First ever atomic bomb test — plutonium implosion device (Gadget)
description: The world's first nuclear explosion. The test validated the implosion design used in the Fat Man bomb dropped on Nagasaki. The blast was visible from three states. The crater is still radioactive.

name: Operation Crossroads
purpose: Effects of nuclear weapons on naval vessels — Able (airburst) and Baker (underwater)
description: Two tests against a fleet of 95 target ships. Baker was the first underwater nuclear explosion, producing a massive radioactive lagoon that contaminated the entire target fleet. The tests displaced the Bikini Islanders permanently.

name: Operation Sandstone
purpose: Weapon design improvements — tested new fission bomb cores
description: Three tests (X-Ray, Yoke, Zebra) focused on improving atomic bomb efficiency. Introduced the levitated pit design that significantly boosted yield-to-weight ratio. Multiple DoE films cover this operation from Navy, Air Force, and Army perspectives.

name: Operation Ranger
purpose: First tests at the Nevada Test Site — rapid development of smaller, lighter weapons
description: Five tests in January-February 1951. The first nuclear tests conducted on the U.S. mainland since Trinity. Included the first test of the Mark 6 bomb design.

name: Operation Greenhouse
purpose: Thermonuclear weapon component tests — first boosted fission devices
description: Four tests (Dog, Easy, George, Item). George tested the first thermonuclear fusion boosting — a crucial step toward the H-bomb. Item was the first boosted fission weapon. The 225 kt yield was double what was predicted.

name: Operation Buster-Jangle
purpose: Weapon effects tests — troops maneuvered near ground zero
description: Seven tests including troop maneuvers (Exercise Desert Rock I and II). Troops were positioned as close as 7 miles from ground zero. Also tested nuclear warheads for anti-aircraft missiles.

name: Operation Tumbler-Snapper
purpose: Airdrop testing and weapon effects on structures
description: Eight tests. First series to test actual airdropped weapons. Included civil defense effects tests with houses, mannequins, and military equipment placed at known distances from ground zero.

name: Operation Ivy
purpose: First full-scale thermonuclear test (H-bomb) — Mike shot
description: Two tests: Mike (10.4 Mt) and King (500 kt). Mike was the world's first hydrogen bomb — a cryogenic device weighing 82 tons that vaporized the island of Elugelab. King was the largest pure fission bomb ever tested. The Mike shot was kept secret from the American public for years. Yield: 694 Hiroshimas.

name: Operation Upshot-Knothole
purpose: Weapon development and civil defense effects
description: Eleven tests. Included the first test of a nuclear artillery shell (the M65 280mm Atomic Cannon). The 'Dirty Harry' and 'Harry' tests caused significant off-site fallout that reached St. George, Utah.

name: Operation Castle
purpose: Deployable thermonuclear weapons — dry fuel H-bombs
description: Six tests. Bravo (15 Mt) was the largest nuclear test ever conducted by the United States — 2.5x the expected yield. The error caused catastrophic radiological contamination: a Japanese fishing boat (Daigo Fukuryu Maru) was irradiated, crew members died, and widespread fallout rained on inhabited atolls. The Bravo shot yielded 1,000 Hiroshimas.

name: Operation Teapot
purpose: Weapon effects and civil defense — 'Operation Cue' mock suburb nuked
description: Fourteen tests including the famous 'Operation Cue' civil defense test: a full mock suburban neighborhood built with houses, mannequins, food, and cars was placed at varying distances from the blast and nuked. Also tested nuclear warheads for air defense missiles.

name: Operation Wigwam
purpose: Deep underwater nuclear test — anti-submarine warfare effects
description: A single 30 kt test conducted 2,000 feet underwater, 500 miles off the California coast. Tested the effects of deep underwater nuclear explosions on naval vessels and submarines. The spray dome was over a mile wide.

name: Operation Redwing
purpose: Advanced thermonuclear weapon development — including first airborne H-bomb drop
description: Seventeen tests. Included the first air-dropped thermonuclear bomb (Cherokee, 3.8 Mt). Also tested the Lacrosse, the first surface-to-surface missile nuclear warhead. Massive series covering the full range of U.S. nuclear capabilities.

name: Operation Hardtack
purpose: Largest test series — full spectrum of weapons development
description: Thirty-five tests, the largest single U.S. test series. Two phases: Hardtack I in the Pacific (weapons development, including 9.3 Mt Poplar shot) and Hardtack II in Nevada (weapon effects, safety tests). Included high-altitude tests that caused unexpected electromagnetic pulse effects.

name: Operation Argus
purpose: High-altitude nuclear tests — electromagnetic pulse and radiation belt effects
description: Three secret high-altitude tests conducted in the South Atlantic. Each warhead was launched by rocket and detonated at 100+ miles altitude. Created artificial radiation belts. Kept classified for years. The tests validated theories about EMP that would later affect weapons design.

name: Operation Dominic
location_name: Christmas Island & Johnston Island & Pacific Ocean
purpose: Last atmospheric test series — response to Soviet resumption of testing
description: Thirty-six tests in 1962. The largest atmospheric test series since Hardtack. Included the Starfish Prime high-altitude test (1.4 Mt at 400 km altitude) which created an artificial aurora visible from Hawaii and knocked out streetlights on Oahu. The last atmospheric nuclear test was conducted on November 4, 1962. Eight months later, the Limited Test Ban Treaty banned atmospheric testing forever.
